title: The Shock Development Problem
speaker: Tristan Buckmaster
affiliation: Princeton University; Member, School of Mathematics
abstract: A fundamental problem in the theory of compressible fluid is to provide a precise description of shock formation and development from smooth initial data. In this talk, I will present recent work in collaboration with Drivas, Shkoller and Vicol on the shock development problem for the 2D compressible Euler under azimuthal symmetry.

proof_stages.label: Euler shock-development formulation
proof_stages.summary: The talk sets up the full compressible Euler equations, the entropy and sound-speed variables, and the geometric target: smooth evolution to a codimension-two preshock followed by a codimension-one shock and weaker outgoing discontinuities.

proof_stages.label: Admissible regular-shock framework and prior results
proof_stages.summary: Formation results, Rankine-Hugoniot jump conditions, entropy admissibility, entropy/vorticity creation, convex-integration nonuniqueness, and earlier development theorems are reviewed to justify working with full Euler regular shock solutions rather than arbitrary weak solutions.

proof_stages.label: Azimuthal reduction and Riemann invariants
proof_stages.summary: Under azimuthal symmetry the full Euler system is rewritten in variables for radial velocity, angular velocity/sound speed, entropy, and specific vorticity, then diagonalized into Riemann invariants with characteristic speeds lambda_1, lambda_2, and lambda_3.

proof_stages.label: Theorem and weak-discontinuity structure
proof_stages.summary: The main theorem is stated: an open class of smooth azimuthal data forms a first C^{1/3} preshock and then has a unique short-time regular shock development with asymptotic jump laws, weak contact discontinuities, and weak refraction waves.

proof_stages.label: Preshock asymptotics via Lagrangian labels
proof_stages.summary: A precise preshock expansion is derived by following lambda_3 and lambda_2 characteristics, observing that singularity occurs when the Lagrangian map has vanishing first and second derivatives, and inverting the resulting cubic degeneracy to obtain fractional C^{1/3} profiles.

proof_stages.label: Approximate developed shock and characteristic geometry
proof_stages.summary: Using the preshock expansion, the speaker constructs an approximate post-preshock solution, computes approximate jumps through Rankine-Hugoniot conditions, and describes the interacting lambda_1, lambda_2, and lambda_3 characteristic curves that must cross the shock and weak discontinuities.

proof_stages.label: Fixed point, higher regularity, and good unknowns
proof_stages.summary: The exact shock development is obtained by a contraction on the shock and weak curves; higher regularity and the weak-discontinuity descriptions are recovered by characteristic/Lagrangian estimates and by introducing good unknowns that remove derivative loss.

examples.name: Jet shock-wave picture
examples.note: A physical jet/shock image is used to make concrete the idea that shocks occur when disturbances overtake the local sound speed.
examples.engagement_reason: the jet picture is used only as an illustrative image of shock waves forming, with no analysis or construction carried out on that jet

examples.name: One-dimensional Burgers/gas-dynamics comparison
examples.note: Audience-prompted comparisons with Burgers and one-dimensional gas dynamics illustrate why the normal shock dynamics are essentially one-dimensional and why the theorem is simpler in pure 1D.

examples.name: Artificial-viscosity density movie
examples.note: A computed density plot with artificial viscosity shows a shock line growing from a preshock point and visually reveals the small bumps later identified with weak discontinuities.

examples.name: Convex-integration Riemann-data nonuniqueness
examples.note: Riemann-data examples of entropy-admissible nonunique weak Euler solutions motivate restricting attention to regular shock solutions with controlled hypersurface singularities.
examples.engagement_reason: the convex-integration Riemann-data example is cited as a known nonuniqueness phenomenon, with only a brief description of the discontinuous data and no construction run through
